title: Temporal anchor alignment candidate
notes: Thirty-ninth-round mutation generated from repeated perfect-tense pressure cards and constrained by the narrative-present and modal-perfect-by-now examples. It tests whether tense/aspect value, modal temporal inference, temporal anchor expression, temporal-anchor fit, current relevance, continuative intervals, experiential frames, and narrative perspective explain contrasts that broad constructional frame fit can only annotate. It is unscored and has no scoped-module label because it was built from the current temporal cards.

edges.rationale: Tense/aspect value is an operator-like value when the repertoire treats it as a contrastive temporal anchoring instruction.
Temporal-anchor fit depends partly on the selected tense/aspect value.
Temporal expressions such as yesterday, since, recently, and one morning last year provide contextual warrant for whether a tense/aspect value fits.
A current relevance relation warrants present-perfect anchoring where the repertoire licenses it.
A continuative interval relation warrants the present-perfect-plus-since frame and undercuts a wholly-before simple-past analysis for the same intended reading.
An experiential frame can make an otherwise excluded definite past-time expression fit when already contributes the relevant comparison.
A narrative-present frame can make present-tense morphology fit a past story-time anchor by shifting viewpoint rather than event time.
A modal inference frame can license will plus perfect with a by-now anchor without treating English will as future tense.
For tense/aspect cases, temporal-anchor fit is part of constructional frame fit rather than a generic discourse frame fact.
Temporal-anchor fit provides warrant for licensing in the relevant tense/aspect repertoire.
Recoverable event meaning can raise reported acceptability without licensing the tense/aspect-anchor combination.
Lower licensing for a tense/aspect-anchor combination predicts greater pressure to repair or reformulate.
Licensing provides contextual warrant for grammaticality attribution without being identical to the attribution.
Reported acceptability provides noisy contextual warrant for grammaticality attribution.
Task framing changes whether respondents treat a temporal-anchor mismatch as grammar, meaning, style, or repair trouble.
